planningGuidelinesTemplate:
# Context for the short-form video
Inputs you must read before planning visuals and writing the XML plan:
- Topic of the short form video: {{topic}}
- Hook for the short form video: {{selectedHook}}
- Plain text script that is narrated out-loud for the short form video: {{textScriptPath}}
- Exact narration transcript used for TTS/alignment: {{transcriptPath}}
- Forced-alignment JSON: {{alignmentPath}}
- Captions/subtitles JSON (for timing/context only; do NOT copy it into XML): {{captionPlanPath}}

# XML Artifact
You must write the final XML to the exact path below, with YAML front matter followed by raw XML only:
{{xmlScriptPath}}

Required XML schema:
```xml
<video>
  <script>Full spoken narration for the entire short-form video.</script>
  <timeline>
    <visual id="visual-1" label="Hook setup" start="0.00" end="1.20" cameraZoom="0.05">
      <image id="hook-setup" characterDriven="false">
        <prompt>Describe the generated image for this visual in extreme detail, including camera positioning, framing, the focal point, and other other details. </prompt>
      </image>
    </visual>
    <visual id="visual-2" label="Reuse hook image" start="1.20" end="2.40" imageId="visual-1" cameraZoomStart="0.02" cameraZoomEnd="0.08" />
    <visual id="visual-3" label="Derived closeup" start="2.40" end="4.00">
      <image id="derived-closeup" basedOn="visual-1" characterDriven="false">
        <prompt>Describe a NEW image generated from the earlier visual-1 image as a reference.</prompt>
      </image>
    </visual>
    <visual id="visual-4" label="Indicator" start="4.00" end="5.40" visualType="motion_graphic">
      <motionGraphic templateId="good-bad-indicator">
        <timing item="icon" at="4.25" />
        <timing item="text" at="4.40" />
        <timing item="rule" at="5.00" />
        <arg name="indicatorType">good</arg>
        <arg name="text">Train the start, not the strain</arg>
      </motionGraphic>
    </visual>
  </timeline>
</video>
```

# Core-concepts: Images, Motion Graphics, and Camera Effects
There are two types of visuals:
1. Images
2. Motion Graphics

Additionally, "camera effects" can be applied to image visuals only (not to motion graphic visuals). Currently, there is only one camera effect available: camera zoom.

## XML semantics for visuals
- Visuals should be defined within the "timeline": <timeline><visual>
- <timeline><visual> entries should describe visuals: label, start/end timing, imageId for image visuals, inline <motionGraphic> for motion graphics, visualType, and camera position/zoom/motion when applicable.
- Reusing the exact same image asset = multiple image <visual> entries with the same imageId. Motion graphics are not reusable; each motion_graphic visual gets its own inline <motionGraphic>.
- Give each <visual> a concise label attribute. The label attribute is only used when displaying rendered visuals in the dashboard webapp, so the user can easily identify the beat on the visual timeline. It is not used to actually render visuals.
- Make sure there are no "timing" gaps between two visuals, meaning each visual's end time should be the same as the next visual's start time.

## Images
Images are what they sound like: images. These images will be generated by an AI image generation model. 

### Image prompts
When defining images, you'll need to specify the prompt that will be sent to the AI image generation model. 

Image prompts should describe the intended image clearly and specifically with a lot of detail, including environment/background details when they matter, and always specifying details like camera framing, positioning, focal points, lighting, etc. They should never include instructions to bake-in text in the generated image.

### Image re-use
When planning an image visual, you can specify that the visual re-uses a previously-defined image visual, instead of requiring an AI image generation model to generate a new image. Image reuse must only point backward in timeline order.



### Image XML Semantics
- Define a generated image directly inside the timeline visual that first uses it: <visual ...><image id="image-id" characterDriven="false"><prompt>...</prompt></image></visual>.
- A later image visual may exactly reuse an earlier image visual by setting imageId to that earlier image id. Reuse must only point backward in timeline order.
- A later image visual may generate a new reference-derived image by putting an inline <image basedOn="earlier-visual-id-or-image-id"> inside the later <visual>. basedOn must only point backward in timeline order.
- Never reference a future visual/image. If an image is needed earlier and later, define it at its first timeline use, then reuse it later.
- Reusing the exact same image asset = multiple image <visual> entries with the same imageId. Motion graphics are not reusable; each motion_graphic visual gets its own inline <motionGraphic>.

## Motion Graphics
Visuals can also be motion graphics. There is a pre-defined set of motion graphics templates that you can use in your plan (see below). Motion graphics cannot be reused, unlike images.

### Motion Graphic XML Semantics
- Define each motion graphic directly inside its one timeline visual as <visual visualType="motion_graphic" start="..." end="..."><motionGraphic templateId="...">...</motionGraphic></visual>.
- Motion graphics are one-to-one with their <visual>; do not reuse them and do not reference them through motionGraphicId.
- Define each motion graphic directly inside exactly one <timeline><visual> as <visual visualType="motion_graphic" start="..." end="..."><motionGraphic templateId="one_of_the_ids_above">...</motionGraphic></visual>.
- Each motion_graphic visual must have one inline <motionGraphic>; do not reference a separate motionGraphicId asset.
- Configure only the listed fields. Do not write arbitrary HyperFrames, JavaScript, CSS, HTML, or renderer code.
- Use <arg name="fieldName">value</arg> for text/number fields.
- To time a core item's animation-in, add animateIn="absolute_video_timestamp_seconds" on the relevant <item>, <step>, or <line>, or add <timing item="title" at="12.20" /> / <timing item="cause" at="12.35" /> inside the inline motionGraphic. Timings are absolute timestamps in the full video timeline, not seconds relative to the motion graphic visual start.
- Keep every motion graphic item timing within that visual's start/end bounds.
- Only time the controllable animation-in items listed for that template. Items that visually belong together, such as a bar plus its value and label or a pie slice plus its legend row, animate together from the same timing.
- For dataSeries fields, use repeated <item label="..." value="..." displayValue="..." /> inside the motionGraphic.
- For field-specific XML shapes, renderer-specific args, split-sequence options, and valid child nodes, follow the XML instructions and example XML inside each individual motion graphic template block.
- Motion graphics are normal visuals; they must not include captions/subtitles/transcript text unless a configured field explicitly represents ordinary on-slide text. The caption_word_wall template is the only full-screen caption replacement and should not be paired with ordinary bottom captions.
- For image visuals, put an inline <image> inside the first <visual> that uses that image, then use imageId only on later visuals that reuse an earlier image visual. For motion_graphic visuals, do not set imageId or motionGraphicId; put the inline <motionGraphic> element inside the visual instead.

### Motion graphic animation timing:
- Motion graphics may include animation-in timings for template core items.
- Use absolute video timestamps on <item>, <step>, or <line> animateIn attributes, or on <timing item="title" at="absolute_video_timestamp_seconds" /> entries.
- These timing values are absolute timestamps in the full video, not seconds relative to the motion_graphic visual start.
- Every motion graphic timing value must be inside that visual's start/end bounds.
- Time only the controllable items listed in the allowed motion graphic templates block.
- Make sure that the end time of the visual using a motion graphic template is at least 3 seconds after the last animation timing timestamp, so readers have time to read and digest the motion graphic.

### Motion graphic read-along text:
- For motion graphics, displayed template text should generally track the exact narration transcript spoken during that visual's time window.
- Use the approved script, exact narration transcript, forced-alignment JSON, and caption timing context to choose wording and reveal timing.
- Keep titles, lines, steps, chart labels, before/after labels, cause/effect text, and key-value labels close to the spoken words so viewers can read along while listening.
- Shorten or adapt wording when the template needs concise labels, especially charts and compact UI, but preserve the important spoken keywords and meaning.
- Use absolute animateIn timings so each core item appears near the absolute video timestamp where its matching spoken phrase or clause begins.
- This is not a caption/subtitle request: do not dump every sentence into motion graphics or create line-by-line captions. Keep text scannable and purpose-built for the template.

### Motion graphic templates you can use:
If no templates are listed below, use image visuals only.
{{motionGraphicTemplates}}

## Camera effects (static and animated camera zooms)
There is one camera effect available to you that you can apply to visuals: camera zooms. Camera zooms can be applied only to image visuals (not motion graphics). There are two types of camera zooms: 
1. Static (the image visual has the same static zoom for the entire duration of the visual)
2. Animated (the image visual has an animated camera zoom effect, e.g. it zooms in over the duration of the visual, or zooms out over the duration of the visual)

### XML semantics for camera zoom
#### Static zoom
Add the `cameraZoom` attribute to a <timeline><visual> tag to add a static zoom (not directly on the <image> element). The attribute value should be a number indicating the zoom amount. 1.0 means no zoom. Anything more than 1 indicates a zoom with some percent value (e.g. 1.2 means 20% zoom)

#### Animated zoom
Add the `cameraZoomStart` + `cameraZoomEnd` attributes to a <timeline><visual> tag to add an animated zoom (not directly on the <image> element).

# Rules for planning engaging visuals
- Don't ever use a motion graphic visual as the first visual in a video
- Ensure there is a visual change at least every 3 seconds across the timeline.
- What constitutes a "visual change":
    a. The image asset displayed changes from visual-to-visual, or
    b. If the same image asset is displayed back-to-back, the camera zoom changes across the visuals. In order to be considered a sufficient visual change, the camera zoom delta between the two visuals must be at least a 0.2 delta differential. Meaning `cameraZoom="1"` to `cameraZoom="1.2"` is considered a sufficient change. If one or both of the visuals uses an animated camera zoom instead of a static camera zoom, then the delta between the first visual's `cameraZoomEnd` and the second visual's `cameraZoomStart` must be at least a 0.2 delta differential.
    c. The visual displayed is a motion graphic. Motion graphics, as their name suggests, contain motion throughout, so the entire time a motion graphic is playing, there are visual changes on the screen. Motion graphics therefore can and should be played for longer than 3 seconds, so that viewers have enough time to digest the motion graphic. HOWEVER, if a motion graphic has animation timings that you can control, the 3 second rule applies to those timings -- try to have no more than a 3 second gap between timings when possible.
- Camera zoom animations should never zoom in by more than a 0.3 zoom amount between the `cameraZoomStart` and `cameraZoomEnd` values.
- Use camera zooms to increase visual changes and engagements.
- Use psuedo-animations to depict actions when possible. For example, if a short-form-video script talks about dribbling a basketball, you could define two asset images: one where the ball is being hit by a hand, and one where the ball is hitting the ground. Then, you could define visuals that display those two asset images and toggle back-and-forth between them to psuedo-animate the action/movement.
- Define B-roll asset images and visuals when appropriate.
- Use motion graphics when appropriate.
- Time motion graphics so that the transition from an image to a motion graphic, and the transition from a motion graphic to an image, happen on a sentence boundary (when one sentence ends and another sentence begins). Transitions from a motion graphic to another motion graphic don’t need to necessarily be aligned with a sentence boundary, however.
- Make sure that visuals make sense in the context of what is being said at the same time in the video.
- Be very cautious about the duration of motion graphics visuals. Make sure that the duration of visuals using motion graphics are long enough so that each animation has time to appear, plus at least a couple of seconds after so that the viewer can digest the final state.
- Mixup the types of visuals as much as the written text script for narration logically allows. When it makes sense to based on the transcript, mixup character-driven visuals, non-character-driven visuals (like environments, images of objects, B-roll), and motion graphics (as well as types of motion graphics used).

# Reviewing the XML artifact
Before completing your work, spend at least one iteration cycle reviewing the XML artifact and making sure it follows the guidelines explained here, and make any improvement that you identify before completing this task.
promptTemplate:
# Goal
Plan the visuals that should be displayed on screen for a short-form video and write the plan as an XML artifact.

If an XML artifact for planning visuals for this video already exists, do NOT read it or base any visuals in your new XML artifact off existing XML artifact. You are creating a totally new XML artifact for planning visuals.

---

{{planningVisualsGuidelines}}


# Output contract
- Write directly to the xml-script.md path above.
- Include YAML front matter with status: needs review, agent: workflow, and suitable title/tags.
- After writing, read the file back and verify it exists on disk.

Project directory: {{projectDir}}
revisePromptTemplate:
# Goal
Revise the existing visuals plan that should be displayed on screen for a short-form video and write the plan as an XML artifact.

An XML artifact has already been written for the visuals plan for this short-form-video, located at: {{xmlScriptPath}}

Your goal is to update that visuals plan based on the revision notes below.

- If xml-script.md already exists, read it first.
- The regenerated Plan Visuals XML must make meaningful body-level changes from the existing XML.
- Rewriting the same XML with only front matter/status/timestamp changes is invalid.
- Existing artifact body state: {{existingXmlBodySummary}}

## Revision notes: 
{{revisionNotes}}

---

{{planningVisualsGuidelines}}

# Output contract
- Write directly to the xml-script.md path above.
- Include YAML front matter with status: needs review, agent: workflow, and suitable title/tags.
- After writing, read the file back and verify it exists on disk.

Project directory: {{projectDir}}
